metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Across domains the same pattern repeats: bold, confident predictions followed by spectacular, avoidable failures. The 1970s productivity paradox showed computers everywhere yet no measurable economic growth, confounding forecasters. Some models succeed: Hanson's 1981 statistical forecast only slightly underestimated warming, an excellent result. The deepest lesson from Tohoku is that treating a disaster as impossible is exactly what makes it catastrophic.

artifactLines: Across domains the same pattern repeats: bold, confident predictions followed by spectacular, avoidable failures.
The 1970s productivity paradox showed computers everywhere yet no measurable economic growth, confounding forecasters.
Some models succeed: Hanson's 1981 statistical forecast only slightly underestimated warming, an excellent result.
The deepest lesson from Tohoku is that treating a disaster as impossible is exactly what makes it catastrophic.

beats.narration_text: The recurring pattern of bold predictions and spectacular failures, from the productivity paradox to Tohoku's 'cannot possibly go wrong' reactor, made me want to separate the forecasts that worked from those that didn't. Hanson's 1981 climate model that slightly underestimated warming intrigues me as a rare success. Can you (1) extract the common features distinguishing the accurate forecasts from the failures, (2) analyze why the Tohoku assumption of impossibility was so catastrophic in probabilistic terms, and (3) propose one rule that would have caught the worst prediction?
